data.sentence: Okay, I think we've got everyone now — can you all hear me okay?
Yeah, loud and clear.
Good here.
Yep, I'm on.

Alright, so — I want to just dive right in because honestly we don't have a lot of time and there's a lot to cover. The outage last week was bad. I don't think any of us are going to sugarcoat that. Six hours of zero threat monitoring visibility is not something our customers are just going to brush off, and our competitors are absolutely going to use this against us.

Yeah, I've already seen it happening. I had two accounts reach out to me directly asking if we had a formal post-mortem they could share internally, and one of them — Hartfield Financial — mentioned that SentinelShield had already been in contact with their procurement team.
Of course they have. That's — I mean, that's exactly what I'd do too if I were them, but still. It's frustrating.

Same thing on my end. I've got a prospect, Clearvale Systems, mid-market, they were this close to signing — and now their CTO is asking pointed questions about our reliability track record. And I didn't have great answers for him, which is not a position I want to be in.
And what are the competitors actually saying? Like are they sending specific messaging or is it more just, hey, we heard you had some trouble?

From what I can gather, SentinelShield is going in with a pretty polished pitch around their multi-region redundancy story. They're positioning it as, um, you know, 'we were built for this, our architecture doesn't have single points of failure' — which, I mean, honestly Tom, that's landing because it's hitting on exactly what went wrong with us.

Look, I'm not going to sit here and pretend the architecture wasn't a problem. It was. The event ingestion pipeline had a clear single point of failure and it cascaded. We know what happened. We've already deployed the redundant processing nodes and the circuit breaker pattern is in place. But yeah — the damage from a perception standpoint, that's real and it's going to take time.
How long do you think before we can point to, like, meaningful uptime numbers post-fix? Because that's what customers are going to want.

I mean, realistically — we need at least 30, 60 days of clean data before we can say anything with confidence. And even then, some customers are going to want third-party validation, not just us saying we're fine.
That's a long time in a competitive sales cycle.
It is. And Brian, what's the support side looking like right now? How many tickets are still open related to the outage?

So we had — ugh — we had a spike of about 340 tickets come in during and immediately after the outage. We've closed most of them, but there are still about 40 open, mostly from customers who want detailed impact reports, like specifically which alerts they missed during the window, what events weren't processed. And a handful of those customers are, uh, pretty heated. I had one escalation yesterday where the customer's security team is claiming they had an actual incident during the outage window and they're saying our lack of visibility may have delayed their response.
Oh, that's — okay, that's a liability issue. Is legal aware of that one?
Yes, I looped them in this morning. We're handling it carefully.

Good. Okay. So, let's talk competitive positioning because that's really why we're on this call. James, Daniel — what are you hearing beyond SentinelShield? Are VaultEdge or CyberNova showing up in these conversations?

VaultEdge has come up a couple of times but honestly more in the backup and recovery conversation than detect, which makes sense — that's their strength. The Hartfield situation is really squarely about Detect, so it's SentinelShield that's the immediate threat there.

Yeah, and for Clearvale, the CTO actually mentioned CyberNova as well — which surprised me a little. They don't usually show up in the mid-market space at that price point. But apparently CyberNova reached out proactively with some kind of competitive displacement offer. I don't know the specifics but — the timing is not a coincidence.
Of course it's not. They've been watching the news feeds. All of them have. And we would do the same thing — I'm just annoyed we're on the receiving end of it right now.
Can we talk about what our counter-narrative actually is? Because right now I feel like we're reactive and I don't have a clean story to bring back to Clearvale.
That's fair. Tom, can you give me something concrete that James and Daniel can take into customer conversations? Like what's actually different now?

Yeah, so — here's what I can say with confidence. The root cause was identified within a couple of hours of the incident starting. We had the fix scoped within 24 hours. Redundant processing nodes are live, the circuit breaker pattern is deployed, and we've now got automated failover testing on a weekly cadence that we didn't have before. That's real. That's not just spin.

Okay, that's good. That's something. But I think we also need to be honest with customers that this was a significant failure and we're taking it seriously, rather than just trying to gloss over it with technical jargon. Customers can smell a cover-up story from a mile away.

Completely agree with Brian on that. The transparency piece is actually a differentiator if we do it right. Most vendors try to minimize, and if we lead with a really candid post-mortem — like genuinely owning what went wrong — that can actually build trust. It doesn't feel that way right now but it can.

I hope you're right about that, Megan, but I've got to be honest — when I'm sitting across from a CTO who's already been called by two competitors, a transparency narrative is a tough sell in that moment. They want to know it won't happen again, and I can't guarantee that.

Nobody can guarantee that. SentinelShield can't guarantee it either, they've had outages too — it was, what, eighteen months ago they had that authentication service issue? I think it was over four hours.

Yeah, use that. That's a legitimate point — every platform has incidents, the question is how you respond and what you've built to prevent recurrence. I'll work with marketing to get some battlecard updates out this week. The current ones are obviously not reflecting any of this.
The battlecards honestly haven't been updated in like, four months anyway. That's been a frustration for a while. No offense, Megan.
No, you're — you're right, and I own that. That's on us. It's something we've deprioritized and this situation is kind of exposing why that was a mistake.

For what it's worth, one thing that might actually help us in the near term — and this is sort of looking ahead — is the Comply v2 launch coming up. If we can position the Detect recovery story alongside something forward-looking like the compliance reporting upgrades, it at least shows customers we're shipping and investing, not just in damage control mode.
That's actually a smart angle, James. When does Comply v2 go GA, is it still early April?
April 7th is the date, assuming nothing slips. Tom?
We're on track. I don't anticipate any delays on that one. The on-demand SOC 2 and HIPAA reporting features are basically done, we're in final QA.

Okay, good. So let's make sure we're sequencing this right for the accounts that are at risk. We want to get in front of them with the post-mortem and remediation story now, and then have a follow-up touch in early April when Comply v2 drops. That gives us two meaningful reasons to engage rather than just one awkward apology call.
That makes sense. I'll reach out to Hartfield early next week and see if I can get a call with their CISO. I think going above the procurement level is the right move there.
Yeah, do that. And Daniel, for Clearvale — do you think the deal is still salvageable or are we in real trouble there?

Honestly? I think it's 50-50 at this point. The CTO is open to hearing us out but he's clearly been spooked. If we come in with a really strong story and maybe some kind of SLA commitment that's better than what we've offered previously, I think we have a shot. But if we fumble the next conversation, I think we lose it.

Alright. Let's not fumble it. Brian, can you make sure that anyone from sales who needs a detailed technical summary of the incident and the remediation has it by end of day Monday? Something they can actually share with customers, not just internal-facing.
Yeah, I'll coordinate with Tom's team and have something ready. I'd say Monday EOD is doable.

Good. Alright, I think we have a plan — it's not a great situation but we know what we're dealing with. Let's just make sure we're communicating internally as things develop. If anyone hears of more competitive activity from SentinelShield or CyberNova specifically, flag it in Slack immediately, don't wait for the next sync.
Agreed. Thanks everyone.
Thanks, talk soon.
Yep, thanks.
Thanks all.
